items.title: ColumnTransformer for mixed tabular features
items.scenario: A churn pipeline has nominal `plan_type`, ordinal `tenure_band` (0–6 months, 6–12, …), and numeric `monthly_spend`. The team builds one `ColumnTransformer`: `OneHotEncoder` on plan, explicit `OrdinalEncoder` categories on tenure bands, and `StandardScaler` on spend—wrapped with logistic regression in a single `Pipeline` saved to disk.
items.explanation: Different columns need different encoders; one fitted transformer block keeps train and inference aligned and versioned with the model.

items.title: Cardinality-driven switch from one-hot to frequency encoding
items.scenario: An ads click model starts with one-hot on `publisher_id` (8,000 levels) and training RAM spikes. The team replaces it with frequency encoding: each row gets the training-set count (or proportion) of its publisher, with unseen publishers mapped to zero—one column instead of 8,000.
items.explanation: High cardinality breaks the one-hot default; frequency encoding compresses levels while staying leakage-safe if counts are computed on train only.

items.title: Rare merchant bucketing before target encoding
items.scenario: Fraud detection has 40,000 `merchant_id` values; 35,000 appear fewer than ten times in training. Engineers keep the top 500 merchants by volume and map the rest to `other`, then apply cross-validated target encoding on the bucketed column.
items.explanation: Bucketing stabilizes statistics for thin groups before target encoding—reducing variance without skipping the cardinality problem entirely.

items.title: Native categoricals vs one-hot for gradient boosting
items.scenario: A pricing team trains LightGBM with `categorical_feature` indices on raw integer-coded store regions (20 levels) instead of expanding 20 binary columns. sklearn `HistGradientBoosting` on the same data still uses one-hot via `ColumnTransformer` because it lacks native categorical splits.
items.explanation: Model library capabilities change the encoding workflow—native categoricals can skip one-hot expansion when the library supports ordered optimal splits on categories.
